11	% genus-0 route (route (c)): the characteristic-free abelian-variety rigidity stack. It is
12	% 1:1 with the new upstream Lean file AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean, which
13	% imports AlgebraicJacobian.Genus and is imported by AlgebraicJacobian.Jacobian (breaking the
14	% RigidityKbar -> Rigidity -> Jacobian import cycle so that genusZeroWitness can consume the
15	% keystone directly). The headline thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV is the char-free
16	% replacement (no [CharZero]) for the over-k-bar declaration rigidity_over_kbar of
17	% \cref{chap:RigidityKbar} (which remains in tree as the fallback route (a) artifact and
18	% still carries [CharZero]). The df=0 / H^0(P^1,Omega)=0 / Serre-duality framing is the
19	% fallback (a), NOT the route developed here.
20	
21	The genus-$0$ arm of the Jacobian-witness construction has a \emph{trivial} witness object
22	($J = \Spec k$); its only remaining mathematical content is a rigidity \emph{statement}:
23	every pointed morphism from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve of genus $0$
24	into an abelian variety is the constant morphism at the identity. We prove this
25	\textbf{characteristic-free} --- the protected goal is over an arbitrary field $k$, so the
26	cheaper differential argument (via $H^0(\mathbb P^1, \Omega) = H^0(\mathbb P^1, \mathcal
27	O(-2)) = 0$, hence $df = 0$, hence $f$ constant) is unavailable: it breaks on a
28	characteristic-$p$ Frobenius-descent wall, where a nonconstant $f$ can have $df = 0$. The
29	rigidity route below sidesteps that wall.
30	
31	The minimal chain has four links. The \emph{Rigidity Lemma} (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma},
32	Mumford, Form~I) is an elementary properness/closed-map argument --- it uses neither the
33	theorem of the cube nor any cohomology, and is the entry point for formalisation. The
34	\emph{theorem of the cube} (\cref{thm:theorem_of_the_cube}) enters only as the deep,
35	characteristic-free input that an abelian variety carries no nonconstant image of $\mathbb
36	P^1$ (the single-curve base case of Milne's Proposition~3.10); it is recorded here as a
37	\textbf{deferred} prerequisite with no formal proof yet. Combining the two gives that every
38	morphism $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ is constant (\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}); composing
39	with the classification of genus-$0$ curves (\cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1},
40	$C \cong \mathbb P^1$ over $\bar k$) yields the headline
41	\cref{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}. This chain invokes neither Serre duality nor the FGA
42	$\Pic$ representability engine, so the genus-$0$ arm is fully decoupled from Route~A.
43	
44	\medskip
45	
46	Throughout, $\bar k$ is an algebraically closed field of arbitrary characteristic. An
47	\emph{abelian variety} over $\bar k$ is a complete (proper) irreducible group variety; in the
48	project's Lean encoding $A$ is an object of $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)$ carrying
49	$\mathtt{GrpObj}$, $\mathtt{IsProper}$, $\mathtt{Smooth}$, and $\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}$.
50	
51	\section{The Rigidity Lemma}
52	
53	\begin{lemma}
54

56	[Rigidity Lemma (Mumford, Form I)]
57	  \label{thm:rigidity_lemma}
58	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_lemma}
59	  % SOURCE: [Mumford, Abelian Varieties], Rigidity Lemma (Form I.), Ch.~II \S4, book p.~43 (read from references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 54)
60	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "RIGIDITY LEMMA. (Form I.) Let $X$ be a complete variety, $Y$ and $Z$
61	  % any varieties, and $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$ a morphism such that for some
62	  % $y_0 \in Y$, $f(X \times \{y_0\})$ is a single point $z_0$ of $Z$. Then there is a
63	  % morphism $g \colon Y \to Z$ such that if $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is the
64	  % projection, $f = g \circ p_2$."
65	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma (Form~I), p.~43.}
66	  Let $X$ be a complete variety, and let $Y$, $Z$ be any varieties over $\bar k$. Let
67	  $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$ be a morphism such that for some $y_0 \in Y$ the image
68	  $f(X \times \{y_0\})$ is a single point $z_0 \in Z$. Then there is a morphism
69	  $g \colon Y \to Z$ with $f = g \circ p_2$, where $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is the
70	  projection; that is, $f$ depends only on the $Y$-coordinate, and $g(y) = f(x_0, y)$ for any
71	  fixed $x_0 \in X$.
72	\end{lemma}

109	  Fix any point $x_0 \in X$ and define $g \colon Y \to Z$ by $g(y) = f(x_0, y)$ (the
110	  composite of $y \mapsto (x_0, y)$ with $f$). Since $X \times Y$ is a variety (in
111	  particular reduced and irreducible, so separated and with no embedded structure), to prove
112	  the equality of morphisms $f = g \circ p_2$ it suffices to prove they agree on a non-empty
113	  open subset of $X \times Y$.
114	
115	  Let $U$ be an affine open neighbourhood of $z_0$ in $Z$, put $F = Z - U$ (a closed set),
116	  and set $G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))$. Because $X$ is complete, the projection
117	  $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is a \emph{closed} map (this is the defining property of
118	  completeness/properness); hence $G$, the image of the closed set $f^{-1}(F)$, is closed in
119	  $Y$. The hypothesis $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \subseteq U$ gives
120	  $f^{-1}(F) \cap (X \times \{y_0\}) = \varnothing$, so $y_0 \notin G$. Therefore
121	  $V := Y - G$ is a non-empty open subset of $Y$.
122	
123	  For each $y \in V$ we have $X \times \{y\} \cap f^{-1}(F) = \varnothing$, i.e.
124	  $f(X \times \{y\}) \subseteq U$. Thus the complete (proper) variety $X \times \{y\}$ is
125	  mapped by $f$ into the \emph{affine} variety $U$. A proper connected variety mapping to an
126	  affine variety has image a single point (a global regular function on a complete connected
127	  variety is constant). Hence $f(X \times \{y\})$ is one point, necessarily
128	  $f(x_0, y) = g(y)$. So for all $x \in X$ and $y \in V$,
129	  \[ f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g(y) = (g \circ p_2)(x, y). \]
130	  The two morphisms $f$ and $g \circ p_2$ thus agree on the non-empty open
131	  $X \times V$, which is dense in the irreducible $X \times Y$; as $Z$ is separated they
132	  agree everywhere. \qedhere

186	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma (Form~I), p.~43 (the
187	  dense-open construction is the body of that proof).}
188	  Let $X$ be a complete variety and $Y$, $Z$ any varieties over $\bar k$. Let
189	  $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$ be a morphism, fix a point $x_0 \in X$, and suppose given
190	  $y_0 \in Y$, $z_0 \in Z$ with the \emph{collapse hypothesis}
191	  \[ f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \qquad
192	     \text{(in Lean: } \mathtt{lift}\,(\mathbf 1_X)\,(\mathtt{toUnit}\,X \fatsemi y_0) \fatsemi f
193	     = \mathtt{toUnit}\,X \fatsemi z_0\text{).} \]
194	  Then there is a non-empty open subset $U \subseteq X \times Y$ on which $f$ agrees, as a
195	  scheme morphism, with the collapsed map $\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f$, where
196	  $\mathtt{retract} := \mathtt{lift}\,(\mathtt{toUnit}\,(X \times Y) \fatsemi x_0)\,
197	  (p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y)$ is the ``$(x, y) \mapsto (x_0, y)$'' endomorphism. Concretely
198	  $U = X \times V$ with $V := Y - G$, $G = p_2(f^{-1}(Z - U_0))$ for an affine open
199	  neighbourhood $U_0$ of $z_0$, and on $U$ one has $f(x, y) = f(x_0, y)$.
200	
201	  \medskip
202	  \noindent\textbf{The collapse hypothesis is load-bearing.} The non-emptiness of $V$ is exactly
203	  the assertion $y_0 \notin G$, and this holds \emph{because} $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\}
204	  \subseteq U_0$ keeps the slice over $y_0$ inside the affine $U_0$, away from $F = Z - U_0$. A
205	  version of this lemma \emph{without} the collapse hypothesis is false: for $X = Y = Z$ and
206	  $f = p_1$ the first projection, $f$ and $\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f \colon (x, y) \mapsto x_0$
207	  agree on no non-empty open (the agreement locus is contained in $\{x = x_0\}$, which has empty
208	  interior in the irreducible $X \times Y$). Therefore the formal target \emph{must} carry the
209	  collapse hypothesis as an explicit antecedent; dropping it (as an earlier decomposition
210	  silently did) makes the deferred goal unsatisfiable.
211	
212	  \medskip
213	  \noindent\textbf{Formalization note: the base field is algebraically closed.} The prose
214	  already takes $\bar k$ algebraically closed, but the formal statement must \emph{encode} this:
215	  the cohomology-free proof of the slice-constancy bridge (below) needs each per-closed-point
216	  residue field $\kappa(y)$ to be $\bar k$ itself, so that a proper integral slice has global
217	  sections $\Gamma = \bar k$ and maps to a single $\bar k$-point of the affine $U_0$. Accordingly
218	  the formalization carries $[\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]$ on this lemma (in addition to
219	  $[\mathtt{Field}\ \bar k]$), and propagates the same instance to \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma} and
220	  its core helper \texttt{rigidity\_core}. This costs nothing downstream: the consumers
221	  \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant} and \cref{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} (Lean
222	  \texttt{rigidity\_genus0\_curve\_to\_grpScheme}) \emph{already} assume
223	  $[\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]$ --- the base-field variable is literally named $\mathtt{kbar}$.
224	\end{lemma}

227	% $g(y) = f(x_0, y)$. Since $X \times Y$ is a variety, to show that $f = g \circ p_2$, it is
228	% sufficient to show that these morphisms coincide on some open subset of $X \times Y$. Let
229	% $U$ be an affine open neighbourhood of $z_0$ in $Z$, $F = Z - U$, and $G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))$;

239	  Let $U_0$ be an affine open neighbourhood of $z_0$ in $Z$, put $F = Z - U_0$, and set
240	  $G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))$. Because $X$ is complete, the projection $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is a
241	  \emph{closed} map, so $G$ (the image of the closed set $f^{-1}(F)$) is closed in $Y$. The
242	  collapse hypothesis $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \subseteq U_0$ gives
243	  $f^{-1}(F) \cap (X \times \{y_0\}) = \varnothing$, hence $y_0 \notin G$; this is the only place
244	  the collapse hypothesis is used, and it is essential, for $V := Y - G$ is non-empty precisely
245	  because $y_0 \in V$. For each $y \in V$ the proper variety $X \times \{y\}$ is mapped by $f$
246	  into the affine $U_0$, hence to a single point, necessarily $f(x_0, y)$. So for all $x \in X$,
247	  $y \in V$, we have $f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)(x, y)$, i.e.\ $f$ and
248	  $\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f$ agree on the non-empty open $U := X \times V$. (In the Lean
249	  encoding the two slice-constancy bridges are the closed-map step from properness and the
250	  ``proper-into-affine has single-point image'' step; see the docstring of
251	  \texttt{rigidity\_core}.)

254	  \noindent\textbf{Formalization notes (the concrete char-free Mathlib route).} The textbook
255	  proof above is realised in Lean by three pieces. They cite Mathlib lemma names as the chosen
256	  route; none uses cohomology, and the relative Stein-factorisation framing is deliberately
257	  \emph{avoided} (see below).
258

260	  the axiom-clean helper \texttt{snd\_left\_isClosedMap} (iter-158). It is obtained from
261	  \texttt{IsProper.toUniversallyClosed} on $X$, base-changed across the canonical pullback square
262	  to make $p_2$ universally closed, using that the monoidal projection \emph{is} the chosen
263	  pullback projection: \texttt{Over.snd\_left} rewrites $(\mathtt{snd}\,X\,Y).\mathtt{left}$ to
264	  $\mathtt{Limits.pullback.snd}\ X.\mathtt{hom}\ Y.\mathtt{hom}$ exactly. This step is already
265	  proven.

268	  $X \times \{y_0\}$ over the $\bar k$-rational point $y_0$ is exactly the image of the slice
269	  section $s \colon X \to X \times Y$ is formalised \emph{without} the residue-field /
270	  \texttt{Triplet} / tensor-product machinery (that fine \texttt{carrierEquiv} layer is the wrong
271	  granularity and is to be avoided). One pastes the identity square for the section against the

273	  \texttt{IsPullback.of\_horiz\_isIso}; the section identities $s \fatsemi p_1 = \mathbf 1$ and

276	  layer of \texttt{PullbackCarrier}, namely
277	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pullback.image\_preimage\_eq\_of\_isPullback}. This needs no
278	  $[\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}]$.

281	  ``Proper connected slice into affine $\Rightarrow$ single point, glued to a morphism equality on
282	  $U$'' is realised by a \textbf{global-sections + per-closed-point} route, \emph{not} by any
283	  relative Stein-factorisation or proper-pushforward $f_*\mathcal O = \mathcal O$ statement: the
284	  latter is a genuine Mathlib gap (no Stein factorisation, no $H^0$ base change), and route~(c)
285	  exists precisely to avoid coherent cohomology, so it must \emph{not} be attempted. The concrete
286	  stack is: for each closed point $y \in V$ one has $\kappa(y) = \bar k$ (alg-closed, finite type);
287	  the slice $X_y$ is proper integral, so its global sections form a field
288	  (\texttt{isField\_of\_universallyClosed}) module-finite over $\bar k$
289	  (\texttt{finite\_appTop\_of\_universallyClosed}), and algebraic closedness kills the finite

292	  $\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f$ agree on each closed slice. Closed points are dense in the
293	  finite-type $U$ (\texttt{closure\_closedPoints} / the Jacobson-space property of

295	  the reduced-source / separated-target rigidity \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'}

297	  packaged ``morphisms agreeing on a dense set of closed points are equal'' hom-extensionality:

299	  ``agrees at every closed point'' into one dominant probe must be assembled from the pieces above

301	  cohomology. It is the residual $\sim 1$--$2$ iteration prover task.

318	  % SOURCE: [Mumford, Abelian Varieties], Theorem (of the cube), Ch.~II \S6 ``The theorem of the cube: I'', book p.~55 (read from references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 66)

332	\section{Morphisms from $\mathbb P^1$ to an abelian variety are constant}
333	
334	\begin{proposition}
335	
336	\leanok
337	[A morphism $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ is constant]
338	  \label{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}
339	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.morphism_P1_to_grpScheme_const}
340	  \uses{thm:rigidity_lemma, thm:theorem_of_the_cube}
341	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proposition 3.10 (specialised to a single $\mathbb P^1$ factor), \S I.3, doc p.~20 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 26)
342	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "PROPOSITION 3.10. Every rational map $\alpha \colon V \dashrightarrow A$
343	  % from a unirational variety to an abelian variety is constant."
344	  % SOURCE QUOTE (unirational, same page): "A variety $V$ over an algebraically closed field
345	  % is said to be unirational if there is a dominating rational map $\mathbb A^n
346	  % \dashrightarrow V$ with $n = \dim V$; equivalently, if $k(V)$ can be embedded into
347	  % $k(X_1, \ldots, X_n)$ (pure transcendental extension of $k$). A variety $V$ over an
348	  % arbitrary field $k$ is said to be unirational if $V_{k^{al}}$ is unirational."
349	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Proposition~3.10 (case $V = \mathbb P^1$).}
350	  Let $\bar k$ be algebraically closed and $A$ an abelian variety over $\bar k$. Then every
351	  morphism $f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ is constant: $f(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k})$ is a
352	  single $\bar k$-point.
353	\end{proposition}
354	
355	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. We may suppose that $k$ is algebraically closed. By assumption
356	% there is a rational map $\mathbb A^n \dashrightarrow V$ with dense image, and the composite
357	% of this with $\alpha$ extends to a morphism $\beta \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \cdots \times
358	% \mathbb P^1 \to A$. An induction argument, starting from Corollary 1.5, shows that there are
359	% regular maps $\beta_i \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A$ such that $\beta(x_1, \ldots, x_d) = \sum
360	% \beta_i(x_i)$, and the lemma shows that each $\beta_i$ is constant."
361	\begin{proof}
362	  \uses{thm:rigidity_lemma, thm:theorem_of_the_cube}
363	  \leanok
364	  This is the single-factor ($d = 1$) instance of Milne's Proposition~3.10, where the
365	  unirational variety is $\mathbb P^1$ itself. We isolate the genuine content.
366	
367	  \textbf{Reduction to a base point.} Translating in the group $A$, we may assume $f(\infty) =
368	  0_A$ for a chosen point $\infty \in \mathbb P^1$. The map $f$ is a morphism out of the
369	  complete nonsingular variety $\mathbb P^1$, so there is no indeterminacy to resolve (a
370	  rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety is everywhere defined, Milne
371	  Theorem~3.2; here $f$ is already a morphism).
372	
373	  \textbf{The base case is where the depth lies.} The constancy of $f$ is the statement that
374	  an abelian variety carries no nonconstant image of $\mathbb P^1$ --- ``$A$ contains no
375	  rational curves''. Over an arbitrary field this does \emph{not} follow from
376	  \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma} alone: the Rigidity Lemma collapses a \emph{product}
377	  $X \times Y \to Z$ when one coordinate axis is already collapsed, but for a single map
378	  $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ no coordinate axis is collapsed a priori (the difference map
379	  $(x, y) \mapsto f(x) - f(y)$ on $\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1$ is constant on an axis only
380	  if $f$ is already constant). The characteristic-free input that supplies the missing fact
381	  is the theorem of the cube (\cref{thm:theorem_of_the_cube}): it forces the additive
382	  structure of $\Pic$ and, with it, the triviality of the line bundles a nonconstant
383	  $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ would produce, so that no such nonconstant morphism exists. (In
384	  characteristic $0$ one may instead pull back an invariant differential and use
385	  $H^0(\mathbb P^1, \Omega) = 0$; that argument fails in characteristic $p$, which is exactly
386	  why the cube is the committed characteristic-free engine.)
387	
388	  \textbf{Conclusion.} Granting the base case, $f$ is constant with value $f(\infty) = 0_A$
389	  (before the normalising translation, the constant value is the original $f(\infty)$). For
390	  the multi-factor statement of Proposition~3.10 (a general unirational $V$), one writes
391	  $\beta(x_1, \ldots, x_d) = \sum_i \beta_i(x_i)$ via the additive decomposition (Milne
392	  Corollary~1.5, itself a corollary of \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}) and applies the base case to
393	  each $\beta_i \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A$; we need only the $d = 1$ case here.
394	\end{proof}

398	  The Rigidity Lemma (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}) is genuinely used in the surrounding
399	  machinery --- it yields Milne's Corollary~1.5 (additivity of $\mathrm{Hom}(- , A)$ over a
400	  product of complete pointed varieties), which drives the multi-factor induction of

410	\section{A genus-$0$ curve over $\bar k$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1$}
411	
412	\begin{proposition}
413	
414	\leanok
415	[Genus-$0$ curve $\cong \mathbb P^1$ over $\bar k$]
416	  \label{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
417	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
418	  \uses{def:genus}
419	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne, Algebraic Geometry], Example IV.1.3.5, Ch.~IV \S1 (Riemann--Roch), doc p.~297 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314)
420	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Example 1.3.5. Recall that a curve is rational if it is birational to
421	  % $\mathbf P^1$ (I, Ex. 6.1). Since curves in this chapter are complete and nonsingular by
422	  % definition, a curve $X$ is rational if and only if $X \cong \mathbf P^1$ (I, 6.12). Now
423	  % using (1.3) we can show that $X$ is rational if and only if $g = 0$. We already know that
424	  % $p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0$ (I, Ex. 7.2), so conversely suppose given a curve $X$ of genus 0.
425	  % Let $P, Q$ be two distinct points of $X$ and apply Riemann-Roch to the divisor
426	  % $D = P - Q$. Since $\deg(K - D) = -2$, using (1.3.3) above, we have $l(K - D) = 0$, and so
427	  % we find $l(D) = 1$. But $D$ is a divisor of degree 0, so by (1.2) we have $D \sim 0$, in
428	  % other words $P \sim Q$. But this implies $X$ is rational (II, 6.10.1)."
429	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, Algebraic Geometry, Example~IV.1.3.5.}
430	  Let $\bar k$ be algebraically closed and $C$ a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
431	  over $\bar k$ with $\genus(C) = 0$. Then $C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.
432	\end{proposition}
433	
434	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the verbatim Example IV.1.3.5 text in the statement block above
435	% (Hartshorne states the classification and its proof inside the single Example; the proof is
436	% the second half beginning ``We already know that $p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0$ \ldots'').
437	\begin{proof}
438	  \uses{def:genus}
439	  \leanok
440	  \emph{Proof sketch (Riemann--Roch level; the formalisation of this step is itself a
441	  sub-build --- Mathlib has no Riemann--Roch for curves).}
442	
443	  Over the algebraically closed field $\bar k$ every closed point is $\bar k$-rational, so $C$
444	  has at least two distinct $\bar k$-points $P \neq Q$ (a smooth proper geometrically
445	  irreducible curve over $\bar k$ is infinite). With $\genus(C) = \dim_{\bar k}
446	  H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0$, apply the Riemann--Roch theorem to the degree-$0$ divisor
447	  $D = P - Q$. Since $\deg(K - D) = 2g - 2 - \deg D = -2 < 0$ one has $l(K - D) = 0$, so
448	  Riemann--Roch $l(D) - l(K - D) = \deg D + 1 - g$ gives $l(D) = 0 + 1 - 0 = 1$. Thus the
449	  degree-$0$ divisor $D$ is linearly equivalent to an effective divisor, forcing $D \sim 0$,
450	  i.e.\ $P \sim Q$. A curve possessing two distinct linearly equivalent points is rational
451	  (Hartshorne II.6.10.1: $P \sim Q$ for $P \neq Q$ yields a degree-$1$ map to $\mathbb P^1$),
452	  and a complete nonsingular rational curve is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1$ (Hartshorne I.6.12).
453	  Hence $C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.
454	\end{proof}

460	  divisor class group machinery at this level, and no $\mathbb P^1$-classification of

502	  $\varphi \colon C \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$. The composite

\begin{lemma}
[Bridge~2: slice-constancy on a saturated open mapping into an affine]
  \label{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine}
  \uses{lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}
  % SOURCE: [Mumford, Abelian Varieties], Rigidity Lemma (Form I.), Ch.~II \S4, book p.~43 (read from references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 54)
  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "For each $y \in V$, the complete variety $X \times \{y\}$ gets mapped by
  % $f$ into the affine variety $U$, and hence to a single point of $U$. But this means that for
  % any $x \in X$, $y \in V$, $f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g \circ p_2(x, y)$, and this proves our
  % assertion." [Mumford, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma, p.~43.]
  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma (Form~I), p.~43 (the
  ``for each $y \in V$, the complete slice maps into the affine, hence to a single point'' step).}
  Let $\bar k$ be algebraically closed and let $X$, $Y$, $Z$ be objects of
  $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)$ with $X$ proper, $X \times Y$ geometrically irreducible and
  reduced, and $Z$ separated. Let $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$ be a morphism, fix a $\bar k$-point
  $x_0 \colon \mathbf 1 \to X$, and write $\mathtt{retract} := \mathtt{lift}\,
  (\mathtt{toUnit}\,(X \times Y) \fatsemi x_0)\,(p_2)$ for the ``$(x, y) \mapsto (x_0, y)$''
  endomorphism. Suppose:
  \begin{itemize}
    \item $U \subseteq X \times Y$ is a $p_2$-\emph{saturated} open, i.e.\ $U = p_2^{-1}(V_{\mathrm{set}})$
      for a set $V_{\mathrm{set}} \subseteq Y$ (so $U$ contains the whole fibre $X_y$ over each of
      its points);
    \item $U_0 \subseteq Z$ is an \emph{affine} open;
    \item $f(U) \subseteq U_0$, i.e.\ $f$ maps $U$ into the affine $U_0$.
  \end{itemize}
  Then $f$ and $\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f$ agree on $U$ as scheme morphisms:
  $U.\iota \fatsemi f = U.\iota \fatsemi (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}
  This is the formalization realization of Mumford's step ``for each $y \in V$, the complete
  variety $X \times \{y\}$ gets mapped by $f$ into the affine variety $U$, and hence to a single
  point of $U$''. We realise it by a \textbf{global-sections + per-closed-point} route that is
  \emph{cohomology-free}; in particular the relative Stein-factorisation / proper-pushforward
  $f_*\mathcal O = \mathcal O$ framing is a confirmed Mathlib gap (no Stein factorisation, no
  $H^0$ base change) and is \emph{deliberately avoided}. The argument has two steps.

  \medskip
  \noindent\textbf{Step 1 (per-closed-slice constancy).} Fix a closed point $y \in V_{\mathrm{set}}$.
  Since $\bar k$ is algebraically closed and $Y$ is of finite type over $\bar k$, the residue field
  is $\kappa(y) = \bar k$. Because $U = p_2^{-1}(V_{\mathrm{set}})$ is saturated, $U$ contains the
  \emph{entire} fibre $X_y \cong X$ over $y$; hence $f$ maps the proper integral slice $X_y$ into the
  affine $U_0$. By $\mathtt{isField\_of\_universallyClosed}$ the global sections $\Gamma(X_y)$ form a
  field, module-finite over $\bar k$ by $\mathtt{finite\_appTop\_of\_universallyClosed}$, and
  algebraic closedness collapses the finite extension to give $\Gamma(X_y) = \bar k$. A morphism into
  the affine $U_0$ is pinned by its ring map on global sections ($\mathtt{ext\_of\_isAffine}$), so the
  restriction of $f$ to $X_y$ factors through a single $\bar k$-point of $U_0$ --- necessarily the
  point $f(x_0, y) = (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)(x, y)$. Thus $f$ and $\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f$
  agree at every closed point of $U$.

  \medskip
  \noindent\textbf{Step 2 (globalisation over dense closed points).} The closed points are dense in
  the locally-of-finite-type $\bar k$-scheme $U$ ($\mathtt{closure\_closedPoints}$ / the Jacobson-space
  property). To turn ``$f$ and $\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f$ agree at each closed point'' into a
  single morphism equality on $U$, we assemble the closed points into one dominant probe --- the
  coproduct $\coprod_{x \in \mathrm{closedPoints}\,U} \Spec \kappa(x) \to U$, whose range is dense ---
  and feed it to the reduced-source / separated-target rigidity handle
  $\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'}$ (the same handle $\mathtt{rigidity\_core}$ uses).
  This yields the morphism equality $U.\iota \fatsemi f = U.\iota \fatsemi (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)$
  on all of $U$. The ``agrees on a dense set of closed points $\Rightarrow$ hom-equality'' connective
  is the one piece Mathlib does not package directly (it supplies only the single-dominant-morphism
  $\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}$); building the dense-range witness over the closed points is a small
  bespoke step, \emph{not} cohomology. \qedhere
\end{proof}
